title: The problem — direct access
instruction:
Right now, anyone can set `account.balance = -9999`.

That is dangerous. Real bank accounts don't let you set the balance directly.

Run this. Notice how easy it is to corrupt an account by direct access.
starter:
class BankAccount:
    def __init__(self, owner, balance):
        self.owner = owner
        self.balance = balance  # fully public

account = BankAccount("Alex", 1000)
print(account.balance)  # 1000
account.balance = -9999  # no error! completely broken
print(account.balance)  # -9999
successMessage: Anyone can set balance to anything. This is the problem encapsulation solves — control access so invalid states are impossible.

title: _ convention — signal private
instruction:
Python's convention: prefix with `_` to signal "this is internal, don't touch it from outside".

It is not enforced by Python — it is a social contract with other developers.

Rename `balance` to `_balance` to signal it is internal.

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: _balance says: 'this is internal — access through the provided methods'. Convention, not enforcement. But respected by all Python developers.

title: @property — controlled read access
instruction:
`@property` turns a method into an attribute-style getter:

```python
@property
def balance(self):
    return self._balance
```

Now `account.balance` calls the method without `()`. Clean, readable, controlled.

Add a `@property` for `balance`.

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: account.balance reads like a simple attribute but runs your method. Users of the class don't even know there's a _balance internally. Clean API.

title: @property setter — controlled write access

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: The setter validates before storing. account.balance = -500 triggers your rule. Invalid state is now impossible — the class enforces its own integrity.

title: Read-only property
instruction:
A property with no setter is read-only.

Trying to set it raises `AttributeError: can't set attribute`.

Add a read-only `id` property that returns a formatted ID string.

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: No setter = read-only. The account ID should never change after creation. Properties give you precise control — read-only, write-only, or read-write.

title: Build a validated user class
instruction:
No starter code.

Build a `User` class with validation:
- `name` (property + setter): must be a non-empty string
- `email` (property + setter): must contain '@'
- `age` (property + setter): must be between 0 and 120

Test it by trying to set invalid values.
hint: Use @property and @x.setter with validation in each setter.
successMessage: A fully validated User class. Invalid data is impossible — the setters enforce the rules at assignment time. This is encapsulation at its finest. Chapter 15 done.
